svg.text: Separated Sets, Disconnected and Connected Sets, Components, Connected Subsets of R, and Continuous Functions on Connected Sets

section.data-narration: This is the introduction to the topic. A set is said to be connected if it cannot be divided into two non-empty, disjoint subsets such that each subset is open relative to the closure of the other.

A set X in a metric space is said to be separated if it can be written as the union of two disjoint non-empty open sets A and B. In other words: X = A ∪ B - A ∩ B = ∅ - A and B are non-empty open sets

A set X in a metric space is said to be disconnected if it can be written as the union of two non-empty disjoint open sets A and B. In other words: X = A ∪ B - A ∩ B = ∅ - A and B are non-empty open sets
A set X in a metric space is said to be connected if it cannot be written as the union of two non-empty, disjoint open sets. In other words: X = A ∪ B - A ∩ B = ∅ - A and B are non-empty open sets

Components are subsets of connected sets. A connected subset of R is a set that is connected in the subspace topology of R. Connected subsets of R are sets that are connected in the standard topology of R
section.text: Components and Connected Subsets of R

section.data-narration: A continuous function on a connected set is a function that preserves connectedness. In other words, if a function f is continuous on a connected set X, then the image of X under f is also connected
